title: did:web domain encoding replaces dots with colons, but spec requires percent-encoding
description:
In `did_web()` (did.rs:30-31):

```rust
let domain_encoded = domain.replace('.', ":");
format!("did:web:{domain_encoded}:users:{username}")
```

The did:web spec (https://w3c-ccg.github.io/did-method-web/) requires dots in domain names to be replaced with colons, which is correct. However, the path separator should use colons to represent `/` in the path, and the did:web resolution algorithm maps `did:web:example:com:users:alice` to `https://example.com/users/alice/did.json`.

The test on line 173-175 expects `did:web:example:com:users:alice` which IS correct per the spec (dots→colons). This is fine.

However, the function does NOT percent-encode special characters in the username (e.g. colons, percent signs). If a username contained a colon, the DID would be ambiguous. The username validation in cli.rs restricts to `[a-z0-9_]` which prevents this — but the function itself is `pub` and has no input validation, relying on caller discipline.

Severity: low — the caller validates, but the function's contract is implicit.
